observations.recommendation:
1.16 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the (a)
OIC-Municipal Accountant to prepare Subsidiary Ledgers containing the
details of all PPE’s General Ledger balances; (b) Inventory Committee
and GSO to (i) submit the Report on the Physical Count of PPE
(RPCPPE) on or before January 31 of each year; and (ii) ensure that the
inventory reports and the Accounting records are properly reconciled; (c)
OIC – Municipal Accountant and GSO to (i) maintain complete PPE
records such as PPE Ledger Cards (PPELC) and Property Cards for
proper control and monitoring of the agency property and equipment;
and (ii) continue the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances to
improve the reliability of the PPE accounts.

1.17 During the exit conference, the OIC-Municipal Accountant and the GSO
mentioned that they have already conducted a physical inventory of PPE,
however, they were unable to prepare a complete inventory report. They also
mentioned that, in coordination with the Inventory Committee, they have
already started implementing the procedures for the one-time cleansing of PPE
account balances, which will continue in calendar year 2025.

2.11 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the
(a) OIC Municipal Treasurer to (i)) continue maintaining updated Real
Property Tax Account Register (RPTAR); and (ii) continue preparing
and submitting to the Municipal Accountant a certified list of taxpayers
and the amounts due thereof at the beginning of the year; (iii) employ
additional measures to increase collections of RPT/SET Receivables as
described in Sections 254 and 256 of RA 7160 and (b) Municipal
Accountant to continue to recognize RPT Receivables and SET
Receivables based on the said certified list of taxpayers and tax due
submitted by the Municipal Treasurer.

2.12 During the exit conference, the Municipal Treasurer stated that the low
collection rate was due to uncollected prior year RPT/SET Receivables.

3.21 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the (a)
BPLO to: (i) strictly enforce the submission of Audited FS or in its
absence, the Sworn Statement of Gross Receipts/Sales or a copy of ITR
from taxpayers for the proper computation of deficiency tax, if any; (ii)
be more strict in the implementation and enforcement of the Municipal
Revenue Code; (b) Municipal Treasurer to examine and verify the books
of accounts and other pertinent records of any business establishments, as
warranted; and (c) prioritize the completion and approval of the updated
Local Revenue Code.

3.22 During the exit conference, the BPLO stated that in CY 2025 Audited
Financial Statements were strictly required from medium to large businesses
and Sworn Statement of Gross Receipts/Sales from small businesses.

4.9 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the (a)
OIC-Municipal Human Resources and Management Officer (MHRMO)
to furnish the Accounting Office with the computed total money value of
leave credits of all employees that will be the basis of the latter in
recording the Leave Benefits Payable in the books of accounts; and (b)
OIC-Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Budget
Officer to take coordinated actions to increase revenue collections and
allocate sufficient budgetary provisions to facilitate the recognition of
expense and liability accounts related to employee benefits.

4.10 During the exit conference, the OIC-MHRMO stated that there was no
pending terminal leave payable. The OIC-Municipal Accountant also stated
that they will set up the Liability Account for Employee Leave Benefits during
the three-year transitory period.

5.10 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the
designated GSO to (a) procure insurance policy covering all insurable
assets of the Municipality; and (b) prepare and submit the Property
Insurance Form (PIF) in accordance with Item 5 of COA Circular No.
2018-002 dated May 31, 2018.

5.11 During the exit conference, the GSO stated that they are currently working on
reconciliation of PPE accounts, therefore, they can only comply with the
Property Insurance Law after the said reconciliation. A representative from the
Budget Office also confirmed the availability of Budget for Insurance
Premiums for CY 2025

7.9 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the (a)
Local Development Council through the Municipal Planning and
Development Officer (MPDO) to continue following the provisions of DBM,
DOF and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 1, dated November 4,
2020; and (b) Municipal Accountant to submit the requisite documents
for further verification/validation of the transactions in accordance with R.A.
No. 9184 or the Government Procurement Reform Act and its Revised
Implementing Rules and Regulations (RIRR) and COA Circular No. 2023-
004 dated June 14, 2023.

8.9 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to (a) continue
its efforts of substantially complying with the guidelines on the
appropriation, utilization, accounting, and reporting of the LDRRMF
following the rules and regulations under RA No. 10121 or the Philippine
Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Act of 2010 and COA Circular
No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012; and (b) consider the formulation
and implementation of Programs, Projects, and Activities (PPAs) to
utilize the STF balances from CY 2020 to CY 2023 for disaster risk
reduction and management activities of the LDRRMC.

9.13 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the
GFPS to (a) continue to allocate at least 5% of the total annual budget for
the implementation of GAD-related activities as required in Section 36 (a)
of RA 9710 or the Magna Carta of Women; and (b) employ the
Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines (HGDG) tool in the
identification and cost attribution of GAD related regular PPAs to GAD.

9.14 During the exit conference, the GFPS stated that they employed HGDG Tool
by sampling for two major projects among 29 GAD Projects. He also stated
that they will comply with the recommendation for the following year.

10.6 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the
OIC – Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the prescribed timelines
of reportorial requirements of the Commission.

10.7 During the exit conference, the OIC-Municipal Accountant stated that they are
currently implementing strict compliance with the timeliness of the
submission of monthly reports and financial transaction documents to COA
Office.

11.6 We recommended and Management agreed to adhere with the timely and
intact remittance of taxes withheld as required under RMC No. 23-2007
and BIR Tax Revenue Regulation No. 10-2008 dated March 23, 2007 and
July 8, 2008.

11.7 During the exit conference, the OIC-Municipal Accountant stated that the
December 31, 2024 balance was already settled in January, 2025.

12.5 We recommended and the OIC - Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Treasurer agreed to continue their efficient performance on the proper
withholding and prompt remittance of GSIS premium contributions and
loan amortizations.

13.5 We recommended and the OIC – Municipal Accountant and the
Municipal Treasurer agreed to continue adhering with the provisions of
RA No. 9679.

14.4 We recommended and the Management agreed to continue its practice of
intact and timely remittances of employees’ and employer’s contributions
due to PhilHealth in accordance with Section 18 of the IRR of the National
Health Insurance Act of 2013 and PhilHealth Circular No. 1, s. 2014.

14.5 We recommended and the Management also agreed that they carefully
prepare the monthly remittance lists and payments to avoid over-
remittance.

We commended the Management, particularly the MAO and the Municipal
Treasurer’s Office (MTO), for the prompt remittance of the taxes withheld during
the current year to the BIR. This notwithstanding, we recommended that
Management continuously take responsibility to comply with the withholding and
timely remittance of taxes in conformity with RR No. 2-98 and RMC No. 23-2007 on
the prompt remittance of the taxes due to the BIR.

We commended the Management for its compliance with the provisions of
R.A. No. 9679 and its IRR on the proper deduction of Pag-IBIG contributions of its
personnel and the remittance thereof within the prescribed period and recommended
the continued observance of the requirements of the Act to ensure sustained
compliance and avoid any penalties or delays.

We commended the Management for its compliance with the provisions of the
Revised IRR of the National Health Insurance Act of 2013, particularly on the proper
deduction and timely remittance of PhilHealth contributions of its personnel. We
recommended the continued observance of these provisions to uphold the welfare and
benefits of the employees.

We commended the Management for consistently maintaining their good
practice of religiously observing the prescribed deadlines on the withholding of
premiums/deductions and the respective remittance thereof to the GSIS in
compliance with the GSIS Act of 1997.

We commended Management for its compliance with regulations governing
the granting, utilization, and recording of CAs issued to SDOs. However, we still
recommended providing stringent measures in the liquidation of the CAs and
granting of additional CAs only upon the liquidation of previous/related CAs.
Moreover, we also recommended designating other SDOs to accommodate some of
the CAs and prevent overlapping ones.

We recommended that management require the OIC-MA to re-assess whether
there is a probability of cash inflow based on income from the delinquent stall owners
and recognize the receivables from the said unpaid space rentals to ensure the
accuracy and reliability of the FS.

We recommended that management:
a. instruct the MT to (i) intensify collections by undertaking effective revenue
collection strategies such as periodic sending of the notice of delinquencies
and tax bills by personal or mail service and coordination with barangay
officials and national and local offices that have access to the addresses of
delinquent taxpayers; and (ii) avail of all legal and administrative remedies
allowed under R.A. No. 7160; and
b. enjoin the OIC-MA and MT to trace and reconcile the discrepancy in the
recorded RPT/SET Receivables vis-à-vis the summary of RPT/SET
Delinquents to facilitate the fair presentation of these accounts in the FS.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. OIC-MA to (i) conduct a thorough review of the Inventory accounts and
reconcile them with the records of the concerned Offices; (ii) derecognize
items that have already been consumed or distributed to end-users in a
timely manner; and
b. MT and other concerned offices to (i) promptly prepare and submit the
SSMI, RIS, and distribution lists to the MAO for adjustments; and (ii)
reconcile the RPCI with inventory accounts in the SL to reflect the correct
inventory of supplies.

We recommended that Management consider the following remedial
measures:
a. prioritize the conduct of one-time cleansing of PPE accounts;
b. direct the Acting-General Service Officer to (i) submit a copy of
registration with the LTO and the insurance record of all motor vehicles
owned by the Municipality, if there is any; and (ii) maintain complete,
detailed PCs to ensure that property records are properly reconciled with
the accounting records; and
c. consider hiring additional manpower under GSO to facilitate the
safekeeping and maintenance of records of all PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Management:
a. enjoin the MA to i) refer always to Circular No. 2015-009 dated December
1, 2015 to be guided in the recording the proper account when reclassifying
completed projects; and ii) record under the CIP accounts the cost of the
projects to the period it relates and the corresponding Accounts Payable
thereof; and
b. ensure the commitment of the MEO to timely submit to the MAO a
monthly monitoring report/statement of work accomplishment regarding
the status/ progress of the construction of all ongoing projects to facilitate
the timely recording of CIP in the books.

We recommended that the LCE:
a. instruct the OIC-MA to (i) draw the following accounting entries to correct
the misstatements in the Due to NGAs accounts:
Particulars Dr. Cr.
Construction Materials Inventory ₱2,610,000.00
Due to NGAs ₱2,610,000.00
To correct entries made on the improper closure of the account on the
project titled “Installation of 58 units Household Sanitary Toilets (DOH
Counterpart)
Due to NGAs 1,963,242.33
Trust Liabilities 1,963,242.33
To correct entries made on the 20% share received by the LGU from the
Fire Code Fees.
b. promptly utilize the downloaded fund from LGSF-FALGU to achieve the
much-needed technological enhancement; and
c. ensure that all funds received from SAs are supported with a duly signed
MOA/TA to clearly define the specific guidelines and procedures in the
implementation of individual projects, including the responsibilities of the
Municipality, thereby ensuring timely accomplishment thereof for the
benefit of the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that Management undertake the following courses of
action:
a) instruct the MA to:
i. draw the following accounting entries to correct the misstatements in
Leave Benefits Payable and Government Equity accounts:
Particulars Dr. Cr.
Leave Benefits Payable ₱50,065.93
Government Equity ₱50,065.93
ii. reclassify the non-current portion of the Leave Benefits Payable for
the proper presentation of the account in the Statement of Financial
Position;
b) direct the HRMO to:
i. initiate and prioritize the reconciliation and correction of balances in
the leave cards; and
ii. revisit its process for updating and maintaining leave cards.
Specifically, the revised process should include regularly updating
leave cards and other internal control measures to provide timely and
accurate updates;
c) enjoin the MA and the HRMO to institute a periodic audit of all employees’
accumulated leaves; and
d) set aside additional reserve funds for the accumulated compensated leave
credits as of year-end; henceforth, ensure the adequacy of the funds
provided for the leave credits earned thereafter by including the said
benefits in the annual budget.

We commended Management for ensuring the full implementation of all
priority projects targeted for the year and for complying with Item 3 of DBM-DOF-
DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 04, 2020, for the benefit of the constituents in
general. However, we recommended that Management harmonize the
implementation of LGUs’ priority development projects, in alignment with their duly
approved local development plans, as well as their medium-term and annual
investment programs, to ensure a more cohesive and efficient planning process.

We recommended that the LCE a) direct the LDRRM Officer to (i) include in
the preparation of the LDRRMFIP/AIP a detailed list of programs, projects, and
activities to be funded from the unexpended balance of the prior year’s LDRRMF
ensuring the rationalize the use of the STF; and (ii) ensure for the timely and full
implementation of LDRRM-related PPAs to achieve the fund’s objectives and
maximized its benefits and b) instruct the MA to correct the mis-recording in the
Trust Liability-DRRM account in the TF books, amounting to ₱157,250.00.

We recommended that Management:
a. instruct the HRMO and his staff to (i) maintain an accurate and complete
record of all personnel to reflect the current workforce; (ii) update the list
periodically to account for the new hires, terminations, and role changes;
(iii) review contract terms aligning job descriptions with actual assigned
tasks to ensure clarity and proper role definition; and (iv) check for
consistency in pay rates across similar roles to uphold fairness; and
b. advise the concerned MAO and HRMO personnel to regularly cross-check
employee records to ensure consistency and verify employment status
updates, including resignations, terminations, and new hires; and ensure
all active employees are properly accounted for to prevent errors in salary
disbursements.

We recommended the LCE to:
a. instruct the LGU GFPS to monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs to
ensure that the same are implemented as planned to attain GAD’s goals
and objectives;
b. formulate a more reliable GAD AR to reflect the reasons for the deviations
as well as the factors that have facilitated or hindered the full
implementation of the GAD PPAs; and
c. instruct the GFP to submit the documentary requirements supporting the
GAD AR.

1.9 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory
Committee to prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report on
Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) and to dispose all unserviceable
properties at the earliest possible time pursuant to Section 79 of PD 1445
and Sections 379-381 of RA 7160.
Additionally, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor implement the One-
Time Cleansing guidelines prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated
January 31, 2020 and require the Inventory Committee to plan ahead the conduct
of Physical Inventory to be used in the One-Time Cleansing and ensure that the
RPCPPE will be submitted on time.
Furthermore, implement a regular assessment on possible impairment of the
LGU’s assets in line with IPSAS.

2.7 We recommended that Local Chief Executive direct the Accountant to
review and re-compute the depreciation provided on the Road Network
accounts and effect the necessary adjustments in the financial statements
for the year.

3.14 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, the Sangguniang Bayan and
the members of the Finance Committee:
a. Review and revise the income forecasting methods and ensure that
income projections are based in realistic assumptions and historical
trends.
b. Establish clear monitoring procedures of the inflows of revenue and
flows of cash to regularly assess the realization of projected income and
to adjust accordingly any unrealized revenue.
c. Henceforth, enhance the cash management practices including
establishing contingency plans to address potential cash shortfall and
employ austerity measures on some of its non-essential activities so as
to recover from the cash overdraft incurred in CY 2024.

4.9 We recommended that the LCE take immediate measures to ensure
procurement of comprehensive insurance coverage for all insurable
properties of the LGU, including the vehicles, infrastructure and other
valuable properties. The insurance coverage should be for fire, theft,
natural disasters and other relevant coverage based on the specific risks
faced by the LGU.

Likewise, we recommended that the Inventory Committee review the
existing Inventory List of PPE and identify those that are insurable and
would require insurance coverage. Ensure that the required PIF is
appropriately filled out, updated and accurate, and submitted to COA and
GSIS to facilitate proper insurance documentation.

5.20 To address these concerns and ensure that hiring practices adhere to the
principles of transparency, efficiency, and necessity, we recommended that the
LCE limit the hiring of Job Order workers to indispensable functions only and
pay the daily minimum wage prescribed by the DBM-CSC-COA JC No. 1,
series of 2017 by employing the following measures:
a. Require the HRMO to determine the necessity, and assess the duties and
functions of JO workers, taking into account their expertise, experience,
and capability to handle the position to come up with the ideal number of
personnel to be hired;
b. Require the MPDC and HRMO to align JO hiring with actual workload
demands, ensuring that personnel are engaged only where there is a
legitimate need. Also, to develop a clear and equitable wage structure for
JO personnel to prevent inconsistencies and unjust compensation
practices.
c. Require all departments to submit detailed justifications for JO hiring,
including workload analysis and impact assessments.
d. Provide in the contract of service the specific details or information such as
expected output/service to be rendered, duration of work, office/project
assignment, source of funds, and its daily rate; and
e. Require each JO worker to prepare the accomplishment report as
attachment to their claims, stating therein the actual and specific tasks
undertaken during the period, duly verified and accepted by the
immediate supervisor.

6.9 We recommended that the provision for the HDMF contribution share of
the LGU be provided in the appropriation of the LGU Funds for the JO
workers mandated to be enrolled in the HDMF Membership. Henceforth,
ensure that the LGU withholds and remits the required contribution,
including the LGU share, to the HDMF.

Henceforth, we recommended that the LGU request HDMF to conduct an
orientation session for the HR and/or Payroll Employees to ensure full
understanding and compliance with HDMF membership requirements
and information dissemination with covered employees.

7.9 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal
Engineer to prepare and submit the Program of Work (POW), plans/drawings
and technical specification with detailed breakdown of the cost in all aspect of
the construction/phases of the project, separate from the POW per phases, so as
to ensure that no construction shall be started for portions that are less than
usable.

Likewise, direct the Finance Committee to appropriately allocate funds and
device contingency plan to address potential financial shortfall and/or delays on
the release of grants from other agencies to ensure continuity of the works.

8.11 We recommended to the Local Chief Executive the following:
a. Require the MSWDO, MHO and Municipal Treasurer to coordinate
and determine the available resources of the LGU to ensure that the
recommended assistance are readily available.
b. Coordinate with the Sangguniang Bayan and the Municipal Social
Welfare and Development Office in formulating the implementing
guidelines in managing the AICS Program aligned with the DSWD
guidelines in the financial and/or in-kind aid granted to various
individuals in crisis situation.

9.17 To ensure that gender-sensitive activities/programs are incorporated into
LGU funded projects, we recommend the following:
a. Formulate a gender-responsive budgeting framework to ensure that
gender considerations are incorporated at LGU implemented projects.
b. Establish coordination between the GAD Focal person/Office and other
departments concerned to ensure GAD integration into LGU funded
PPAs.
c. Implement a system for tracking and evaluating the GAD Fund
utilization.
d. Use the GAD Accomplishment Report Template set by the Philippine
Commission on Women.

10.8 We recommended that the LCE direct the MDRRMO to immediately
submit the LDRRMF monthly utilization report for review, ensuring
compliance with the regulations and enhancing transparency and
accountability in the management of disaster-related funds.
Further, we recommended that the MDRRMO observe the prescribed
monitoring and reporting system on the utilization of the MDRRMF.

11.9 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the MPDC, Local
Finance Committee and other officials concerned to:
a. Conduct early procurement planning, improve market studies, and
ensure compliance with RA 9184 to preempt delays in the procurement
and implementation of the projects.
b. Prepare periodic progress reports on fund utilization and project
implementation to facilitate proactive issue resolution.
c. Otherwise, identify alternative priority projects that can be
implemented within the fiscal year for possible realignment to optimize
fund utilization.

12.16 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the recipient
barangays to submit the status report on the implementation of the
requested project to be funded out of the 20% DF.
Henceforth, draw a MOA in every undertaking with the BLGU providing for
the reporting requirements including liquidation reports, implementation
period and return of any unused funds.
Further, we recommended that Finance Committee ensure that all
development projects that are funded out of the 20% DF should comply
with the requirements in DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1.

13.8 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal
Engineer to post billboards in the project site on all implemented
infrastructure projects and/or to update subsequently the tarpaulin
signboards in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-
004.

Further, require the Accountant to ensure that the pictures of the posted
signboards be part of the supporting documents in the payment of progress
billings of infrastructure projects. Also, appropriately disclose complete
information on the status of the Construction-In-Progress account.

14.17 We recommended that the Local School Board observe the fund allocation
and utilization reporting, transparency, and monitoring by employing the
following suggested actions:
a. Enhance the implementation of the information dissemination on how
the SEF is allocated and utilized. Consider the implementation of an
online portal or public dashboard or any social media resources available
in the locality to ensure greater visibility and/or make the allocation and
utilization report accessible to stakeholders.
b. Require the Accountant to render detailed quarterly and annual reports
on the specific use of the SEF which should include the amount spent on
particular programs/activities provided and the outcomes achieved.
c. Mandate the official/committee concerned to submit comprehensive
post-activity reports immediately upon the completion of the funded
program/activity. The report should include the details of the
challenges faced, measurable results, and outcome evaluation metrics
to assess the direct impact of the program/activity.

15.8 We recommended that the Local School Board facilitate regular
consultations and forums from key stakeholders, particularly the teachers,
parents and students to encourage inputs in the identification of the
programmed activities/projects to ensure that the SEF budget is aligned
with the educational needs of the District. Likewise, require all the
members of the LSB to actively participate in the SEF Budget preparation.

We commended the PCF Custodian for compliance with majority of the provisions in Chapter 3, Volume 1 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.
We also recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant strictly follow the Imprest System of recording Petty Cash Fund transactions in accordance with Section 52.1.b, Chapter 3, Volume 1 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs.

The CDP of the Municipality is for the duration for seven years. As such, the Municipal Planning and Development Office (MPDO) regularly ensure that the Fund is being utilized to finance the Municipality’s priority development projects, as embodied in its duly approved local development plan, and medium-term and annual investment programs, which should be harmonized with the Regional Development Plan and the Philippine Development Plan.
The concerned Departments regularly ensure that every construction project have suitable CSHP in accordance with the rules and other orders and issuances issued by the DOLE.
The BAC ensured that every construction project have a suitable CSHP in accordance with the rules and other orders and issuances issued by the DOLE.
Corresponding Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) on payments of infrastructure contracts for CY 2024 revealed that the MA regularly ensure that the surety bond issued by contractors as surety bond for contractors as warranty bonds contain the specific dates of coverage, specifically the date starting from the time of completion of the project and up to one year to its final acceptance, including the expiration date thereof.

observations.recommendation:
a) include in the preparation of the MDRRMFIP/ AIP the list of PPAs to be undertaken out of the unexpended balance of the prior year LDRRMF to rationalize the use of the STF; to be undertaken out of the unexpended balance of the prior year LDRRMF.
(b) strive for the timely and full implementation of the LDRRM-related PPAs to achieve the goals and objective of the fund and the desired benefits; and

For CY 2024, the MA regularly furnished the Report on the Utilization of LDRRMF in the required format on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.

Intensive Information and Education Campaign relative to SWM were conducted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting entries to revert to the Cash-In-Bank and Government Equity accounts the unreconciled amount. Further, we recommended that the Accountant and the Treasurer regularly reconcile their records on the balances of the Cash-In-Bank Account to timely identify any valid reconciling items.
observations.managementAction: Prepared the necessary adjusting entries to revert to the Cash-In-Bank and Government Equity accounts the unreconciled amount. Conducted regular reconciliation of cash-in-bank accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the transfer of the cost of completed projects booked as PPE under the General Fund pursuant to MNGAS. Likewise, we recommended that the corresponding depreciation shall be provided pursuant to IPSAS 17 based on the estimated useful life that shall be provided by the Municipal Engineer, otherwise, use the estimates prescribed under COA Circular 2003-007.
observations.managementAction: The cost of completed PPE was recorded accordingly and the depreciation was duly provided.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher to reclassify completed infrastructure projects from the CIP to the appropriate Infrastructure Asset account and appropriately provide depreciation thereof. Also, direct the Engineering Office and Accounting Office to coordinate the monitoring of project status to timely make the appropriate reclassification of completed projects.
observations.managementAction: The CIP and PPE accounts were reconciled. Implementation of infra projects is regularly monitored and reported.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report on Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) and to dispose all unserviceable properties at the earliest possible time pursuant to Section 79 of PD 1445 and Sections 379-381 of RA 7160. Additionally, implement the One-Time Cleansing guidelines prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 and require the Inventory Committee to plan ahead the conduct of Physical Inventory to be used in the One-Time Cleansing and ensure that the RPCPPE will be submitted on time. Furthermore, implement a regular assessment on possible impairment of the LGU’s assets in line with IPSAS.
observations.managementAction: Inventory taking and identification of the unserviceable PPE and proposal of the appropriate mode of disposal is being conducted. In the meantime, the unserviceable PPE were stored out of the Municipal Building vicinity.
observations.reason: Not Insufficient manpower to conduct the report

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Budget Officer and Accountant to maintain details of the appropriation and utilization of the fund and regularly monitored. Further, ensure that the minimum budgetary and reporting requirements are complied so as to provide accurate information on any amount that may be subject of re-appropriation by the LDRRM Council and Sangguniang Bayan.
observations.managementAction: The record of the appropriation and utilization of the fund is monitored and the minimum budgetary requirement was complied.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive take the following courses of action to ensure the effective implementation of the DRRM Program: a. Regularly review the annual LDRRM Plan, evaluate the implementability of the programmed PPAs, and revise or recommend for re-appropriation, thru the LDRRM Council and SB, if warranted; b. Expressly include the GAD aspect in the LDRRM Plan and Budget; c. Establish a monitoring and evaluation scheme on the implementation of the DRRM PPAs so as to immediately address any hindrances and/or delays; and d. Ensure that the minimum budgetary and reporting requirements are complied.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRM Plan is regularly reviewed and updated as to its PPAs. The utilization of the DRRM implemented PPAs is regularly monitored and reports are complied with.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require all barangays to establish an operational MRF. Likewise, conduct regular inspection and monitoring of the established MRFs to ensure the operationalization of these facilities including waste diversion and effective record management. Further, enforce strict implementation of the Penalties provided in the Municipal ordinances and sanction individuals and entities that continuously violate the prohibited acts contrary to pertinent RA 9003.
observations.managementAction: All Barangays are now having operational and functional MRFs which are regularly monitored including the submission of reports from every Barangay regarding the amount and classification of waste collection. Penalties are served to violators through citation tickets to be paid to the MTO.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the following measures to the Local Chief Executive: a. Instruct the MENRO to strictly look into the proper disposal and segregation of wastes in the Open Disposal Facility and Residual Containment Area operated by the LGU. b. Require the Solid Waste Management Board to devise measures consistent with the provisions of R.A 9003 and its IRR to prevent hazards or risks posed to the health of residents, garbage collectors, and the environment. c. Implement comprehensive monitoring and review processes on the collection and disposal of waste to ensure adherence to waste management regulations and standards and continuous improvement in its practices.
observations.managementAction: MENRO and Solid Waste Management Board conducted inspections, proposed improvements, and enforcement measures. Operational MRFs established in barangays. Monitoring and review processes instituted.
observations.reason: Not Insufficient funds for further implementation and manpower constraints

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Local Chief Executive refrain from approving/allowing the assistance that is not within the Municipality’s responsibility to finance and/or subsidize.
observations.managementAction: The disallowed assistance and/or expenses mentioned in the AOM were no longer approved.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure that National Government Agency (NGA) employees were duly authorized by their mother agency to go on travel and permitted to claim the traveling expenses from the LGU. Henceforth, ensure that all travel orders and itineraries of travel shall be approved by their respective authorized official. Further, the policies and guidelines on claims for traveling expenses be consistently enforced to all employees of the LGU and NGA employees authorized to claim from the LGU funds. Moreover, require the Municipal Accountant to verify thoroughly and review the supporting documents of claims for travel to ensure that only those with valid and proper authority are allowed.
observations.managementAction: Similar claims mentioned in the AOM are no longer approved if supporting documents are not complete. Travel claims are now reviewed and only those authorized are allowed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer to ensure the collection of amounts from the persons liable for the settlement of the disallowed/charged transactions prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-06 and to issue the Notices of Finality of Decision and COA Order of Execution as necessary.
observations.managementAction: Issued demand letters or notices to the liable persons and pursued collection actions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to immediately submit to the Audit Team the missing documents relative to the Notices of Suspension to avoid disallowance in audit pursuant to Section 82 of Presidential Decree 1445.
observations.managementAction: Coordinated with the auditors and submitted the required missing documents to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, the Sangguniang Bayan, and the Treasury, Finance Offices: a. Establish clear procedures for monitoring financial transactions, including regular reconciliations and reviews to identify and promptly rectify discrepancies. b. Review previous years’ transactions and identify the specific purpose and/or sources of the balances under the 20% DF and effect the necessary corrections in the records. c. Prepare the necessary Utilization and/or Liquidation Reports.
observations.managementAction: Utilization Reports were prepared and submitted. Previous years’ charges against the 20% DF were reviewed and adjusted where appropriate. Regular reconciliations are being conducted.

observations.recommendation: a. The Inventory Committee to conduct complete annual inventory taking of PPE using the guidelines enumerated in COA Circular 2020-006 and submit a copy of the RPCPPE/Inventory Report not later than January 31 of each year using the prescribed format in Annex A of the Circular; and b. Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to maintain Property Cards and Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards, respectively.
observations.managementAction: Conducted complete physical inventory taking and submitted RPCPPE. Hard copies of the transactions from prior years were reconciled; ICS/PAR on file with the Treasurer.
observations.reason: The absence of a full time Supply Officer and difficulties in retrieval of old corrupted soft copies delayed full compliance

observations.recommendation: a. Ensure that the PPAs prioritized under the 20% Development Fund are well-planned and implementation-ready so that its implementation within the period for which it is proposed is not hampered; and b. Regularly monitor the status of utilization and implementation of the PPAs identified.
observations.managementAction: The LGU monitored PPAs; prioritized PPAs for implementation and performed follow-up actions. Procurement and implementation actions were initiated in Oct. 2024 and monitored.
observations.reason: Delayed procurement and insufficient funds were among the reasons for non-implementation; actions taken in Oct. 2024

observations.recommendation: a) The LDRRM Officer to formulate and classify the programs under the 70% Mitigation Fund in the LDRRM Plan encompassing the four (4) thematic areas in accordance with Section 3 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1; and b) The LDRRM Officer and other officials concerned to maximize the utilization and implementation of the identified PPAs funded under LDRRMF in order to effectively increase the level of preparedness of the municipality in case of occurrence of calamities and threats.
observations.managementAction: The 70% Mitigation Fund reports were grouped and classified per thematic area. Appropriations that were not implementable were re-appropriated to other eligible PPAs.
observations.reason: Some appropriations still on-going implementation

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer ensure that an ICS was issued to the end-user and appropriately recorded in the stock cards to establish accountability and monitor its whereabouts.
observations.managementAction: Adjusting entries were effected. ICS/PAR is on file with the Treasurer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to submit the supporting details/schedules and to provide adequate and necessary disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: Necessary schedules and disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements were prepared and submitted.

observations.recommendation: a) Monthly Report of Official Travels prepared by the driver concerned and Monthly Equipment Utilization prepared by the equipment custodian summarizing in chronological order his trips or use of equipment, respectively, for the month, duly certified by the driver and approved by the Administrative Officer; b) Report of Fuel Consumption shall be prepared and submitted monthly to the auditor for verification purposes. The report shall show the different types of motor vehicles utilized by the agency during the month, total distance traveled, total fuel consumed and the normal fuel consumption for each vehicle.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that management apply all available legal remedies to recover from the absconded Municipal Treasurer all funds for which he is accountable.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the LCE agreed to  require the (a) OIC – Municipal Accountant to (i) compute/determine the amount of impairment and provide for allowance for impairment for dormant receivable accounts in order to have a fair presentation of the financial statements as required under IPSAS 29; 

(ii) reconcile the difference in the general ledger and subsidiary ledger balances of the Other Receivables account; and

(iii) record the adjustment to correct the double recording of transaction in the Due from Officers and Employees account and Other Receivables account; and

(b) if conditions and circumstances on writing – off the accounts would be present, direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Legal Officer to initiate the procedures for the application of the request under COA Circular 2016-005.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to (a) re-construct from the available documents the schedule/subsidiary ledgers of the liability accounts; and 

(b) conduct reconciliation of the outstanding balances of loans in accounting records with that of the LBP and determine the cause of discrepancy of ₱430,823.55 and recognize book adjustment, if warranted.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the designated GSO to (a) prepare and submit the PIF in accordance with COA Circular No. 2018-002 dated May 31, 2018; and

(b) procure insurance policy to cover all insurable assets of the Municipality.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the GFPS to 

(a) continue to allocate at least 5% of the total annual budget for the implementation of GAD-related activities as required under Section 36 (a) of RA No. 9710 or the MCW; and

(b) employ the HGDG tool in the attribution of costs of regular program/project to GAD.
We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to observe the timelines of reportorial requirements of the COA.

We recommended and the LCE together with the Human Resource Management Officer agreed to 

(a) formulate and issue approved policy guidelines on the hiring of job order promulgating the standards for uniform application; and

(b) refrain from hiring job order whose functions (i) overlap/duplicate those of the regular employees of the Municipality; and (ii) pertain to vacant regular plantilla positions.

We recommended and the Municipality agreed to adhere with the timely and intact remittance of taxes withheld as required under RMC No. 23-2007 and BIR Tax Revenue Regulation No. 10-2008 dated March 23, 2007 and July 8, 2008.

observations.recommendation:
We also recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant strictly follow the Imprest System of recording Petty Cash
Fund transactions in accordance with Section 52.1.b, Chapter 3, Volume 1
of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the (a)
OIC-Municipal Accountant to prepare Subsidiary Ledgers containing the
details of all PPE’s General Ledger balances; (b) Inventory Committee
and GSO to (i) conduct actual physical inventory of all PPE of the
Municipality and submit the Report on the Physical Count of PPE
(RPCPPE) on or before January 31 of each year; and (ii) reconcile the
resultant inventory reports with the Accounting records; (c) OIC –
Municipal Accountant and GSO to (i) maintain complete PPE records
such as PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards for proper control and
monitoring of the agency property and equipment; and (ii) consider the
one-time cleansing of PPE account balances to improve the reliability of
the PPE accounts.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the
OIC – Municipal Accountant to reclassify the Deferred RPT / SET as
Current Liabilities.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the
designated GSO to (a) procure insurance policy covering all insurable
assets of the Municipality; and (b) prepare and submit the Property
Inventory Form (PIF) in accordance with Item 5 of COA Circular No.
2018-002 dated May 31, 2018.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the (a)
Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to regularly reconcile
the Revenue Accounts and make sure to report the correct amount in the
Financial Statements; (b) BPLO to: (i) strictly enforce the submission of
Audited FS or in its absence, the Sworn Statement of Gross Receipts/Sales
or a copy of ITR from taxpayers for the proper computation of deficiency
tax, if any; (ii) be more strict in the implementation and enforcement of
the Municipal Revenue Code; and (c) Municipal Treasurer to examine
and verify the books of accounts and other pertinent records of any
business establishments, as warranted.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the
OIC – Municipal Accountant, the OIC – Municipal Treasurer, and other
officials responsible to strictly observe the prescribed timelines of
reportorial requirements of the Commission.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the
GFPS to (a) continue to allocate at least 5% of the total annual budget for
the implementation of GAD-related activities as required in Section 36 (a)
of RA 9710 or the Magna Carta of Women; and (b) employ the
Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines (HGDG) tool in the
attribution of costs of regular PPAs to GAD.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to require
the (a) Local Development Council (LDC) through the Municipal
Planning and Development Officer (MPDO) to continue following the
provisions of DBM, DOF and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC)
No. 1, dated November 4, 2020; and (b) Municipal Accountant to submit
the complete requisite documents on a timely manner for
verification/validation of the transactions in accordance with R.A. No.
9184 or the Government Procurement Reform Act and its Revised
Implementing Rules and Regulations (RIRR) and COA Circular 2012-001
dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to continue its
efforts to substantially comply with the guidelines on the appropriation,
utilization, accounting, and reporting of the LDRRMF following the rules
and regulations under RA No. 10121 or the Philippine Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Act of 2010 and COA Circular No. 2012-002
dated September 12, 2012.

We commended the Management for its continuous efforts to comply with
R.A. No. 9003 or the Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000.

We recommended and the OIC – Municipal Accountant and OIC –
Municipal Treasurer agreed to continue adhering with the provisions of
R.A. No. 9679, and to effect the remittance of the exact amount of
contributions withheld for a given month.

We commended the OIC - Municipal Accountant and OIC – Municipal
Treasurer for their efficient performance on the proper withholding and
prompt remittance of GSIS premium contributions and loan
amortizations. We also recommended that they carefully prepare the
monthly remittance lists and payments to avoid over-remittance.

We recommended and Management agreed to continue its practice of
intact and timely remittances of employees’ and employer’s contributions
due to PhilHealth in accordance with Section 18 of the IRR of the
National Health Insurance Act of 2013 and PhilHealth Circular No. 1, s.
2014. We also recommended that they carefully prepare the monthly
remittance lists and payments to avoid over-remittance.

We recommended and Management agreed to adhere with the timely and
intact remittance of taxes withheld as required under RMC No. 23-2007
and BIR Tax Revenue Regulation No. 10-2008 dated March 23, 2007 and
July 8, 2008, respectively.

We commended the Management for its substantial compliance with the
provisions of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 relative to the
granting of CA for CF. However, we recommended that the MA/MT ensure full
compliance with the deadline of submission of the CA including supporting
documents for timely audit and the proper use of accounts to record the same.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a) instruct the MA to i) reclassify the Infrastructure Assets totaling
₱7,507,729.64 to Flood Control System, and ii) record under the pertinent
CIP accounts the cost of the projects to the period it relates and the
corresponding Accounts Payable thereof; and
b) ensure the commitment of the MEO to submit to the MAO a monthly
monitoring report/SWA regarding the status/progress of the construction
of all on-going projects to facilitate the timely recording of CIP in the
books.

We recommended that Management return the unexpended balances of
various fund transfers to its respective SAs.

We commended the Management for continued compliance with the
guidelines for utilization of 20% DF under DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1. However,
we still recommended that Management:
a. amend/have a supplemental plan on the CDP and LDIP to include all the
current development projects being implemented during the year which
was not included prior the approval of the said plan;
b. ensure that all completed projects were serving their purpose as soon the
same were determined to be as such; and
c. instruct the (i) MAO to scrutinize the validity period of the performance
and warranty security before payment; (ii) BAC to (a) compel the
contractor to post warranty security to cover structural defects after the
issuance of final acceptance; and (b) require the winning contractor to
submit their DOLE-approved CSHP.

We recommended that the Management direct the LDRRM Officer to (i)
include in the preparation of the MDRRMFIP/AIP the list of PPAs to be undertaken
out of the unexpended balance of the prior year LDRRMF to rationalize the use of
the STF; (ii) strive for the timely and full implementation of LDRRM-related PPAs
to achieve the goals and objective of the fund and the desired benefits; and (iii)
submit the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF within the
reglementary period and the Revised/Updated LDRRMFIP to COA Office for timely
review of the report.

We commended the Municipality for its earnest effort in implementing various
PPAs for their sustainable SWM. However, we still recommended and Management
agreed to:
a) achieve the full operationalization of the MSWMB to readily act on
emerging issues in the implementation of solid waste management and
provide effective and diverse solutions thereto and religiously perform all
the duties and responsibilities required from themselves;
b) provide funding for the determined programs, strategies, and activities for
SWM or source out financing from other government agencies that could
95
provide financial assistance to the Municipality including a salary grade
for the department head of MENRO;
c) provide regular supplies of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) and
appropriate trainings to garbage collectors and other personnel directly
dealing with collection of solid waste as well as covers to waste
compartments of trucks or haulers, and sufficient details for identification
on the body of all motor vehicles used for collection and transport of solid
wastes; and
d) encourage and provide support to activate the established Barangay MRFs
and obliged those who does not have to construct their own, and instruct
all the concerned Barangays to refrain from storing waste at transfers
station beyond 24 hours.

We recommended that the LCE, being the Chairman of the GFPS, undertake
the following courses of actions, through its Focal Person:
a. monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs and evaluate the level and
extent to which the GAD PPAs have become effective and efficient in
addressing GAD issues;
b. create a GAD M&E Team to establish, enhance, strengthen, and/or
maintain a gender-responsive monitoring and evaluation system; and
c. formulate a more reliable GAD AR and observe the deadline for
submission thereof to the AT.

We recommended that Management (a) direct the MA and the MT to
immediately transmit the unsubmitted DVs; and (b) strictly adhere to the prescribed
timeframes on the rendition of accounts and preparation/submission of financial
reports pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting entries to revert back to the Cash-In-Bank and Government Equity accounts the unreconciled amount. Further, we recommend that the Accountant and the Treasurer regularly reconcile their records on the balances of the Cash-In-Bank Account so as to timely effect any valid reconciling items.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the transfer of the cost of completed projects booked as PPE under the Trust Fund to the General Fund pursuant to MNGAS. Likewise, we recommended that the corresponding depreciation shall be provided pursuant with IPSAS 17 based on the estimated useful life that shall be provided by the Municipal Engineer, otherwise, use the estimates prescribed under COA Circular 2003-007.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher to reclassify completed infrastructure projects from CIP to the appropriate Infrastructure Asset account and appropriately provide depreciation thereof.  Also, direct the Engineering Office and Accounting Office to coordinate on the monitoring of project status to timely make the appropriate reclassification of completed projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to conduct regular inventory, inspection and assessment of all the properties of the LGU which should include the identification of the unserviceable PPE and proposal of the appropriate mode of disposal.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Budget Officer and Accountant to maintain details of the record of the appropriation and utilization of the fund. Further, ensure that the minimum budgetary and reporting requirements are complied so as to provide accurate information on any amount that may be subject of re-appropriation by the LDRRM Council and Sangguniang Bayan.

We recommended that the Solid Waste Management Board through the Local Chief Executive require all barangays to establish an operational MRF. Likewise, conduct regular inspection and monitoring of the established MRFs to ensure the operationalization of these facilities including waste diversion and effective record management. Further, enforce strict implementation of the sanctions provided in the Municipal ordinances and RA 9003 against individuals and entities that continuously violate the prohibited acts thereof.

We recommended the following measures to the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct the MENRO to look into the proper disposal and segregation of wastes in the Open Disposal Facility and Residual Containment Area operated by the LGU.
Require the Solid Waste Management Board to devise measures consistent with the provisions of R.A 9003 and its IRR to prevent hazards or risks posed to the health of residents, the garbage collectors, and the environment.
Implement comprehensive monitoring and review processes on the collection and disposal of waste to ensure adherence to waste management regulations and standards and continuous improvement in its practices.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive refrain from approving/allowing assistance that it is not within the Municipality’s responsibility to finance and/or subsidize.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure that National Government Agency (NGA) employees were duly authorized by their mother agency to go on travel and permitted to claim the traveling expenses from the LGU. Henceforth, all travel order and itinerary of travel shall be approved by their respective authorized official.

We further recommended that the policies and guidelines on claims for traveling expenses be consistently enforced to all employees of the LGU and NG employees authorized to claim from the LGU funds. Moreover, require the Municipal Accountant to verify thoroughly and review the supporting documents of claims for travel to ensure that only those with valid and proper authority are allowed.

We recommended for the Local Chief Executive to require the Municipal Treasurer to ensure collection of amounts from the persons liable for the settlement of the disallowed/charged transactions prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-06.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to immediately submit to the Audit Team the missing documents relative to the Notices of Suspension to avoid disallowance in audit pursuant to Section 82 of Presidential Decree 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, the Sangguniang Bayan and the Finance Offices:
Establish clear procedures in monitoring financial transactions, including regular reconciliations and reviews to identify and promptly rectify discrepancies.
Review previous years’ transactions and identify the specific purpose and/or sources of the balances under the 20% DF and effect the necessary correction in the records.
Prepare the necessary Utilization and/or Liquidation Reports.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the OIC - Municipal Accountant to (a) recognize in the respective fund’s books the unrecorded transactions; and

(b) regularly reconcile the balances of the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts to enhance the reliability of the said accounts’ balances.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to 

(a) continue its efforts to substantially comply with the guidelines set forth by RA No. 10121 and NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 2013-1; and

(b) require the OIC- Municipal Accountant/LDRRM Officer to comply with the accounting and reporting for LDRRMF PPAs as provided for under COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We commended the Management for its continuous efforts in complying with the COVID – 19 vaccination program guidelines. We also recommend that the LCE instruct the 

(a) OIC – Municipal Accountant in coordination with the MHO to determine and recognize in the books the full cost of the ending inventory of vaccines as at year-end and to henceforth record future receipts and issuances of the same; and

(b) MHO to continue spearheading the LGU’s vaccination activities until the desired local herd immunity is attained.

We recommended that the OIC – Municipal Accountant and OIC – Municipal Treasurer agreed to continue to be compliant with the GSIS law on the deduction and remittance of the contributions and premiums due to the GSIS.
We recommended and that the OIC – Municipal Accountant and OIC – Municipal Treasurer agreed to continue their adherence to the provisions of RA No. 9679.
We recommended and the OIC – Municipal Accountant agreed to reconcile his records with that of the PhilHealth’s records to settle the difference of PhilHealth accounts.
